petitioner_background: The petitioner is a Chinese acrobat seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in athletics and the arts under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. She performed with the Chongqing Acrobatic Art Troupe in China, specializing in programs including 'Juggling with the Feet,' and arrived in the United States in 2002. The petition was filed on July 14, 2003.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a 'Golden Paris Award' certificate presented to the Chongqing Acrobatic Troupe (not to petitioner individually) for the 'Future World Circus Festival' in 1994, which was a pre-printed fill-in-the-blank form; (2) a 'Silver Prize in the Belgium International Acrobatic Competition in 1997' with no first-hand evidence in the record; (3) a 'Diploma' issued in 2000 from the Organizing Committee of the April Spring Friendship Art Festival, D.P.R. of Korea, stating 'THE GOLD PRIZE IS AWARDED TO [illegible] PARTICIPATING IN TH [sic] APRIL SPRING FRIENDSHIP ART FESTIVAL'; (4) a non-translated, pre-printed 'Diploma of Honor' presented in 2001 by the Kiwanis Club of Monaco with five fill-in-the-blank spaces; (5) non-certified translations of three 'Golden Lion' award certificates (May 1991, September 1995, and October 2000); (6) non-certified translations of award certificates for a Golden Prize and a Silver Prize at the '2nd National Promising Successors Acrobatic Competition' (September 1993) for her 'Juggling with the Feet' program; and (7) a non-certified translation of an award certificate for a paper winning third place at the '4th National Acrobatic Theory Conference.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found none of these qualified as a major, internationally recognized award (like the Nobel Prize, Academy Award, or Olympic Gold Medal) or as lesser nationally/internationally recognized prizes. The Golden Paris Award was given to the troupe, not the petitioner. The Korean diploma had a misspelling ('TH' instead of 'THE') that diminished its evidentiary weight. The Golden Lion Award is purportedly held every four years, yet certificates dated September 1995 and October 2000 create an unresolved discrepancy under Matter of Ho. The National Promising Successors competition was limited to her age group, offering no meaningful comparison with experienced acrobats, and the record unexplainedly shows she received both Gold and Silver Prize at the same competition. None of the award certificates complied with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3)'s translation requirements. The pre-printed fill-in-the-blank form certificates suggested multiple winners. The petitioner failed to show total number of certificates distributed, criteria used, or media coverage. Letters from the Chinese Acrobatic Association alleging national recognition were insufficient. Additionally, there was no evidence of any awards after 2001, suggesting petitioner had not sustained acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted her China Acrobats Association membership card and a letter from the China Acrobats Association listing its membership requirements, which required 'five years in an acrobatic career' and other achievements at the 'provincial level.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the membership requirements did not constitute outstanding achievements. Requirements based on years of experience and provincial-level achievements do not meet the criterion. The evidence did not establish that the petitioner's membership required outstanding achievement or that her admission was evaluated by experts at the national or international level, since evaluation at the local or provincial level does not qualify.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted an incomplete translation of a May 3, 2002 article appearing in Tianfu Morning, which devoted only four sentences to the petitioner. She also submitted promotional flyers from the Chinese Performing Artists of America related to events in Southern California, with only one of the items naming the petitioner.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Tianfu Morning article did not comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) translation requirements and the petitioner was not the primary subject since only four sentences concerned her. Published material must be primarily about the petitioner and printed in professional/major trade publications or major media with significant national or international distribution. The promotional flyers from Southern California events were local materials that did not qualify as major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted letters from the Chairman and Vice Chairman of the Chinese Acrobatic Association and the Head Coach of the Chongqing Acrobatic Troupe, describing the petitioner as a talented performer and mentioning her competitive awards.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO emphasized that regulatory criteria are separate and distinct, so awards evidence cannot also serve as contributions evidence. The letters described the petitioner as accomplished but offered no information on how her individual contributions significantly influenced her field. The issue is not skill level or creativity but whether past endeavors qualify as contributions of major significance. There was no evidence showing the extent of petitioner's influence on professional acrobats. An individual with sustained acclaim should produce ample unsolicited materials reflecting that acclaim, and without extensive documentation showing her work was unusually influential or highly acclaimed, the criterion was not met.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted an incomplete translation of a paper she authored entitled 'Scientific Training is a Must Road for Acrobatic Innovation.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: There was no evidence showing the article was published or that it appeared in major media. The article could not be accepted as evidence because it was not accompanied by a full English language translation as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). There was also no evidence showing the petitioner's article was widely viewed throughout her field as significantly influential.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed eligibility under this criterion based on her acrobatic performances.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO has consistently found this criterion more appropriate for visual artists (such as sculptors and painters) rather than for performers or athletic competitors. Virtually every acrobat 'displays' her work in the sense of performing in front of an audience. In athletic competition or performing arts, acclaim is generally not established by the mere act of appearing in public but rather by attracting a substantial audience. Separate criteria exist for those whose work is in the performing arts, and the petitioner's stage performances would be addressed under the leading role criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a letter from nine of her team members on the Chongqing Acrobatic Art Troupe stating that because of her sports acrobatic foundation, she learned two programs faster and better than all of them, took up the most difficult acts, and served as a lead performer. The letter stated that without her as lead performer, they would not have been able to participate in several major national and international competitions and win major awards.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: While the petitioner may have 'served as a lead performer' for two of the troupe's programs, the evidence was not adequate to establish that her role was any more critically acclaimed than other performers. The record lacked multiple published reviews of her performances to demonstrate significant acclaim throughout China. The record also did not adequately demonstrate that the Chongqing Acrobatic Art Troupe enjoyed a distinguished national or international reputation. The AAO concluded she had not established she performed a leading or critical role for a distinguished organization, or that her involvement earned her sustained national or international acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted an unsigned letter from the Culture Bureau of Sichuan Province stating that she 'has performed more than 1900 times and reached audiences of more than 15,000,000 people.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: This criterion calls for commercial success in the form of 'sales' or 'receipts.' Simply documenting the petitioner's participation in multiple stage performances cannot meet the plain wording of the regulation. The record contained no evidence of documented sales or receipts showing that the petitioner's individual performances drew record crowds, were regular sell-out performances, or resulted in greater audiences than other similar performances that did not feature the petitioner.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to demonstrate receipt of a major internationally recognized award and failed to meet at least three of the regulatory criteria required to establish sustained national or international acclaim. The record was ambiguous regarding her acclaim in China, and there was no evidence she sustained any acclaim after her 2002 arrival in the United States. The evidence was not persuasive that her achievements set her significantly above almost all others in her field at the national or international level, and she had not distinguished herself as a performer to such an extent that she achieved sustained national or international acclaim or placed within the small percentage at the very top of her field.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Several procedural issues were noted: (1) None of the award certificates complied with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) requiring certified complete English translations; (2) the Korean 'Gold Prize' diploma contained a misspelling ('TH' instead of 'THE') that diminished its evidentiary weight; (3) an unresolved discrepancy under Matter of Ho regarding Golden Lion Award dates (certificates five years apart when the award is supposedly given every four years); (4) an unexplained anomaly of receiving both Gold and Silver Prize at the same competition for the same program; (5) a letter from the Culture Bureau of Sichuan Province was unsigned; and (6) multiple translations submitted were incomplete or non-certified.
